content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

May 15, 1995

Richard Doyle
Chief Deputy City Attorney
City of San Jose
151 West Mission Street
San Jose, CA 95110

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-95-126

Dear Mr. Doyle:

This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Mike Ellzey, Executive Director of the San Jose Arena Authority,
under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act.1/ Your request does not address a specific decision pending
before Mr. Ellzey, but rather seeks general guidance regarding
future decisions. Accordingly, we will treat your letter as a
request for informal assistance.2/ The facts set out in this
letter were provided in your request for advice as well as in
subsequent telephone messages.

QUESTION

As the executive director of the San Jose Arena Authority,
may Mr. Ellzey participate in decisions which may increase
patronage to a steakhouse and bar which is the solely-owned
subsidiary of a corporation in which he has an investment
interest?

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. I-95-126
Page 2


CONCLUSION

     Mr. Elizey may not participate in any decisions of the San
Jose Arena Authority if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on the steakhouse
and bar, or on the corporation in which he has an investment
interest.

FACTS

     Mr. Mike Elizey, the Executive Director of the San Jose Arena
Authority ("Authority"), has disclosed an economic interest of 10
percent or greater in a closely-held corporation which owns and
operates Blake's Steakhouse and Bar.  In a telephone message, you
indicated that the value of his interest is approximately $50,000.

     You also indicated in a telephone message that Mr. Elizey
loaned the corporation approximately $22,500.  At the time of
writing the request for advice, Mr. Elizey had not received any
income or other distribution from the corporation.

     Blake's Steakhouse and Bar ("Blake's") is located
approximately one-half mile from the San Jose Arena.  Blake's is
frequented by Arena patrons both before and after events.  Some
matters before the Authority can result in increased activity at
the Arena, and a likely increase in business activity to Blake's.

ANALYSIS

     Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

     An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate
family, or on:

* * *

     (b)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(Section 87103(b))

     "Investment" means any financial interest in or security
issued by a business entity, including but not limited to common
stock, preferred stock, rights, warrants, options, debt
instruments and any partnership or other ownership interest owned
directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official or his
or her immediate family.  (Section 82034.)

File No. I-95-126
Page 3


     In addition, Regulation 18706 provides that an official has a
financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
81700, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on a business entity which is a parent or
subsidiary of, or is otherwise related to, a business entity in
which the official has a financial interest.

     Regulation 18236(a) defines a "parent-subsidiary"
relationship as:

          (a)  Parent-subsidiary.  A parent-subsidiary
     relationship exists when one corporation directly or
     indirectly owns shares possessing more than 50 percent
     of the voting power of another corporation.

                                   (Regulation 18236(a).)

     You indicated that the value of Mr. Ellsey's interest in the
closely-held corporation is $50,000, and that he has loaned the
corporation $22,500.  He has yet to receive any income or other
disbursements from the corporation.  The corporation owns and
operates Blake's.

     Accordingly, Mr. Ellsey may not make, participate in making,
or attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally, on the closely-held corporation or on Blake's.

     Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.  An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.  Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.  (In re: Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

     In order to determine whether the effect of a governmental
decision on an official's economic interest is material, we must
first determine whether the public official's economic interest is
directly involved in the decision and whether the effect of the
decision is material under Regulation 18702.1.

     Regulation 18702.1(a)(2) provides that a decision is material
as to any business entity, in which the official has a direct or
indirect investment of $1,000 or more, which is directly involved
in a decision before the official's agency.  A person or business
entity is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency when that person or business initiates the proceeding in
which the decision will be made by filing an application, claim,
appeal, or similar request, is a named party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or
the official's agency.  (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

File No. I-95-126
Page 4


     Mr. Ellzey's economic interest is not directly involved in
the decisions by the Authority under Regulation 18702.1. We
therefore turn to the regulations applicable to the indirect
effects of the Authority's decisions on the corporation or on
Blake's.

     Whether the indirect affect on a business entity is material
depends on the financial size of the corporation. We have not
received information as to the financial size of corporation in
which Mr. Ellzey holds his investment, but for businesses on the
smaller end of the scale, a decision is deemed material if the
decision will result in an increase or decrease in the gross
revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more, will result in the
business entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $2,500 or more, or will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more.
(Regulation 18702.2(g).) A copy of Regulation 18702.2 is enclosed
for your use in determining which standard applies.

     Accordingly, if any decision before the Authority is likely
to increase or decrease activity to Blake's and such decision
would have a material financial effect on Blake's, or on the
corporation, Mr. Ellzey may not participate in that decision.3/

     I hope this has been helpful to you. If you have any
questions or need to discuss this, please feel free to call me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Jeanette E. Turvill
                                            Political Reform Consultant
                                            Legal Division

JET/jet
Enclosure


_________________________
3/   If you determine that it is reasonably foreseeable that
Mr. Ellzey's economic interests will be materially affected by any
decision of the Authority, Mr. Ellzey could still participate in
the decision if the decision affects 50 percent of all businesses
in the jurisdiction of the authority in substantially the same
manner. It is doubtful, however, that 50 percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction are located one-half mile from the
Arena or would be affected in substantially the same manner as
Mr. Ellzey's interests. A copy of Regulation 18703 is enclosed
for your information.
